codeReview.summary: Reviewed the prior failed review, both hardening feature entries and handoffs, the original failing diff in `e41220b`, the fix diff in `b17471a`, the fix feature transcript skeleton, the `integration-worker` skill, and the relevant shared-state docs. The fix does resolve the original review's trust-reset persistence gap by clearing `runtimeNotificationSeenEventIds` and `runtimeNotificationPendingLaunchTarget` during local trust reset (`apps/mobile/lib/features/pairing/application/pairing_controller.dart:434-442`). It also replaces the prior snackbar-only routing with a `flutter_local_notifications` adapter plus callback/cold-start payload handling (`apps/mobile/lib/features/settings/data/runtime_platform_notifications.dart:57-159`, `apps/mobile/lib/features/settings/application/runtime_notification_delivery_controller.dart:217-236,407-482`). However, the feature still does not make VAL-CROSS-002/008/009 defensible end to end. Notification creation is still driven only by the in-process live websocket subscription started from the controller (`runtime_notification_delivery_controller.dart:217-236,318-320`) and then converted into a local notification (`runtime_notification_delivery_controller.dart:407-457`). There is still no background execution, remote-push, or bridge-to-OS delivery path anywhere under `apps/mobile`, so a suspended or terminated app has no code path that can receive a new bridge event and create the notification in the first place. The new integration coverage proves payload routing only through test doubles and bootstrapped launch payloads (`apps/mobile/integration_test/notification_deep_links_and_dedup_test.dart:101-158,338-356,805-847`), not actual delivery to an app that was backgrounded or dead when the event occurred. So the fix only partially addresses the original failure.

issues.description: The fix still does not implement real background/terminated-app notification delivery. The controller only initializes platform notifications and then subscribes to the live bridge stream inside the running app (`runtime_notification_delivery_controller.dart:217-236,318-320`); `_deliverNotification()` posts a local notification only after that in-process stream receives an event (`runtime_notification_delivery_controller.dart:407-457`). There is no background service, remote push token flow, or other bridge-to-OS delivery path anywhere under `apps/mobile`, so when the app is suspended or terminated at event time there is no code path that can receive the approval/live-activity event and create the notification required by VAL-CROSS-002/008/009. The new tests only inject fake opened payloads / initial launch payloads (`apps/mobile/integration_test/notification_deep_links_and_dedup_test.dart:101-158,338-356,805-847`), which verifies routing after a payload already exists but does not solve or exercise real delivery to a backgrounded or dead app.

Permission denial is treated as if system delivery were available. `_requestNotificationPermissions()` ignores the boolean results from Android/iOS/macOS permission requests (`runtime_platform_notifications.dart:111-127`), and `_initializeInternal()` still marks `_systemNotificationsAvailable` true unconditionally afterward (`runtime_platform_notifications.dart:90-100`). `showNotification()` then returns success whenever `_plugin.show(...)` does not throw (`runtime_platform_notifications.dart:137-159`). If the user has denied notification permission, the controller will suppress its in-app fallback path while the OS still drops the notification, so approval/live-activity updates can disappear silently.

summary: Fail. The fix usefully adds platform-local notification callbacks and clears persisted dedup/launch keys on trust reset, but it still relies on an in-app websocket to create notifications, so it does not actually deliver approval/live-activity notifications when the app is backgrounded or terminated. I also found a new non-blocking regression where denied OS notification permissions are treated as successful delivery availability, which can silently suppress the in-app fallback.
